content:
Navigating Bias and Ethics in Artificial Intelligence

Artificial intelligence (AI) has transformed the way we live, work, and interact. From automated hiring platforms to predictive healthcare models and facial recognition software, AI is increasingly influencing decisions that have profound impacts on society. Yet, as AI’s reach expands, so do concerns about fairness, accountability, and ethical responsibility. One of the most pressing ethical challenges in AI today is bias—systematic errors in decision-making that disproportionately harm certain groups. Addressing this issue is not just a technical problem; it requires thoughtful consideration of ethical frameworks, governance structures, and human oversight.

In this post, we explore the ethical dimensions of AI bias, examine its real-world consequences, and discuss strategies for promoting fairness and justice in AI systems.

Understanding Bias in AI

Bias in AI occurs when algorithms produce outcomes that systematically favor or disadvantage particular groups. This bias can enter AI systems at multiple stages:

1. Data Bias

When training datasets are unrepresentative or contain historical inequalities, AI models inherit and often amplify these patterns. For example, facial recognition systems have been shown to perform poorly on darker-skinned individuals because training datasets overrepresent lighter-skinned faces.

2. Algorithmic Bias

Even with balanced data, design choices in algorithms can produce unfair outcomes. Certain optimization methods may unintentionally favor attributes correlated with privileged groups.

3. User Bias

Human involvement in AI, such as labeling training data or interpreting outputs, can introduce subjective prejudice into the system.

Real-World Example: Bias in Criminal Justice

A striking case highlighting the dangers of biased AI comes from the criminal justice system. In 2016, ProPublica investigated the COMPAS (Correctional Offender Management Profiling for Alternative Sanctions) risk assessment tool used in U.S. courts. COMPAS was designed to predict the likelihood of reoffending. However, the analysis revealed that African-American defendants were disproportionately labeled as high-risk compared to white defendants with similar criminal histories. Such algorithmic bias risks reinforcing systemic inequalities, potentially leading to unjust sentences and loss of liberty. Even more recently, in February 2026, a man in the United Kingdom was wrongfully arrested due to biased facial recognition software used by police. The AI system mistakenly matched his face to that of a burglary suspect hundreds of kilometers away, despite significant differences in appearance. This case illustrates how biased AI can directly impact civil rights and public trust in technology.

Why Ethical AI Frameworks Matter

To mitigate these harms, ethical AI frameworks provide guiding principles to ensure AI development aligns with societal values. Several key AI ethics concepts are central to responsible AI:

1. Beneficence

AI should promote well-being, preserve dignity, and avoid harm. Applications in healthcare or education, for example, should enhance human flourishing rather than exacerbate inequality.

2. Nonmaleficence

Beyond promoting good, AI must avoid causing harm. This includes privacy violations, security lapses, and ensuring that AI does not erode human autonomy or safety.

3. Autonomy

Humans must retain the power to make decisions. AI systems should support, not replace, human judgment, particularly in critical areas like healthcare, legal decisions, or employment.

4. Justice

AI should treat individuals and groups equitably. This principle emphasizes fairness, the elimination of discrimination, and promoting equal opportunities. Frameworks like the Montreal Declaration stress the need to ensure AI development reduces rather than amplifies social inequalities.

5. Explicability

AI decisions must be understandable and accountable. Explainable AI (XAI) allows stakeholders to interpret outputs, identify errors, and assign responsibility when negative consequences arise.

Types of Bias and Their Impacts

Understanding the specific forms of bias can help us identify interventions:

  • Sampling Bias Occurs when datasets fail to represent the diversity of real populations. For example, early facial recognition datasets predominantly featured white males.
  • Representation Bias Results from underrepresentation of certain groups, such as women in medical datasets, leading to poorer diagnostic predictions.
  • Measurement Bias Arises when data collection methods systematically distort reality. Urban-centric surveys may neglect rural populations, skewing results.
  • Interaction Bias – Emerges when AI systems behave differently based on user demographics during deployment.
  • Generative Bias – Seen in AI-generated content, where dominant cultural patterns are amplified, such as portraying CEOs mostly as men.

The consequences of these biases are far-reaching. Biased AI can restrict access to essential services, reinforce stereotypes, and diminish trust in technology.

Mitigation Strategies: Toward Fair AI

Addressing bias in AI requires both technical and ethical strategies. Key approaches include:

Dataset Augmentation

Expanding datasets to better represent diverse populations helps reduce sampling and representation bias.

Bias-Aware Algorithm Design

Incorporating fairness considerations during model development, such as counterfactual fairness, ensures outcomes remain equitable across groups.

User Feedback Mechanisms

Allowing humans to flag problematic outputs can help correct errors and improve system fairness over time.

Regular Audits and Oversight

Continuous evaluation, using both human judgment and automated tools, ensures that biases are detected and addressed.

Interdisciplinary Collaboration

Combining expertise from computer science, ethics, law, and social sciences strengthens the ethical governance of AI systems.

However, these strategies come with challenges. Achieving fairness often involves trade-offs between accuracy and equity, and defining fairness itself is context-dependent. Collecting representative data may raise privacy concerns, while ethical prioritization requires careful consideration of which groups or harms to address first.

Fairness in Practice

Fairness in AI can be approached in several ways:

Group Fairness

Ensures that outcomes are equitable across demographic groups, such as ensuring loan approval rates are similar for different racial groups.

Individual Fairness

Similar individuals receive similar outcomes, regardless of group membership.

Counterfactual Fairness

Considers whether changing a protected attribute (like race or gender) would alter an outcome, allowing for fairness evaluation in hypothetical scenarios.

Applying these fairness concepts is particularly important in high-stakes domains such as healthcare, finance, and law enforcement, where biased decisions can profoundly affect human lives.

The Role of AI Governance and Ethics

Ethical frameworks alone are insufficient without strong AI governance. Governance includes policies, regulations, and oversight mechanisms to ensure accountability. Examples include:

  • Mandated algorithmic audits in public sector AI deployments.
  • Requirements for transparent reporting of AI decision-making processes.
  • Legal frameworks that assign responsibility for AI-induced harms.

Soft ethics—encouraging voluntary ethical practices and organizational culture—also plays a role, especially when formal regulation lags behind rapid technological development.

Looking Ahead: Generative AI and Bias

As generative AI becomes more widespread, addressing bias is increasingly urgent. These systems can inadvertently reinforce dominant cultural norms, perpetuating stereotypes or marginalizing underrepresented voices. Ethical AI development must therefore prioritize:

  • Diverse and inclusive training data.
  • Teams with varied backgrounds and perspectives to minimize blind spots.
  • Transparent and accountable deployment, with mechanisms for correcting harmful outputs.
Conclusion

AI has the potential to enhance human life in countless ways, but bias threatens to undermine its benefits. Ethical frameworks—including beneficence, nonmaleficence, autonomy, justice, and explicability—provide crucial guidance for designing AI systems that are fair, transparent, and socially responsible.

Real-world examples, from COMPAS to biased facial recognition in the UK, highlight the tangible consequences of neglecting bias in AI. Mitigation strategies, including dataset augmentation, fairness-aware algorithms, and interdisciplinary governance, offer paths forward, though challenges remain in balancing accuracy, fairness, and privacy.

Ultimately, responsible AI development requires continuous vigilance, collaboration, and ethical reflection. By embedding fairness into the AI lifecycle and prioritizing human well-being, society can harness the power of AI while minimizing harm and ensuring that technology serves all members equitably.

Sources

Confronting the Challenges of Regulating Artificial Intelligence. Retrieved from https://ecollections.law.fiu.edu/lawreview/vol20/iss1/5/
title: When AI Gets It Wrong

content:
Ethical Considerations in Course Feedback Analysis

Data-driven decision-making is central to improving organizational outcomes, and surveys are a common tool for gathering feedback. However, even seemingly straightforward survey analyses can raise ethical questions, especially regarding privacy, fairness, and transparency. In this post, we’ll examine a scenario involving a post-course survey, applying data science ethics concepts to determine the right course of action while maintaining trust and integrity.

The Scenario: Anonymous Course Surveys

At the end of a training program, participants are asked to complete a 10-question survey. Access to the survey is controlled via a secure URL, and students log in with their organization credentials. The survey prompts participants to enter the course name but not their own name. The description emphasizes that all responses are anonymous, yet executive management intends to use the results to decide which courses need redesign or updates.

As the assigned data analyst, your task is to provide actionable insights for course improvement without violating the ethical principles promised to participants.

Ethical Challenges in Survey Analysis

Several ethical issues emerge in this scenario:

1. Anonymity and Privacy

The survey explicitly states that responses are anonymous. Logging in with a username could technically allow tracking of individual answers, even if the dataset you receive is de-identified. Nonmaleficence, the principle of avoiding harm, is key here: analysts must ensure that promised anonymity is upheld, preventing potential breaches of privacy or misuse of personal data.

2. Transparency and Trust

Participants are making the assumption that their feedback is confidential. If management or analysts link responses back to individuals—intentionally or inadvertently—this could violate trust and reduce participation in future surveys. Ethical data handling requires explicability, meaning that participants and stakeholders understand what the data will be used for and how privacy is protected.

3. Actionable Insights vs. Individual Identification

Executive management wants insights at the course level to inform redesigns. This goal is achievable without identifying individual participants, but there is a temptation to over-analyze small groups, which could unintentionally reveal identities. Here, the principle of beneficence applies: the analysis should provide value without causing harm.

4. Bias and Fairness

Even anonymized survey data can be skewed if certain groups are over- or underrepresented in responses. If insights are drawn from biased data, recommendations for course redesign may unfairly favor or disadvantage certain learners, raising ethical concerns about justice.

Applying Ethical Data Science Concepts

To navigate these challenges, the following ethical concepts guide decision-making:

1. Nonmaleficence (Do No Harm)

Even anonymized survey data can be skewed if certain groups are over - or underrepresented in responses. If insights are drawn from biased data, recommendations for course redesign may unfairly favor or disadvantage certain learners, raising ethical concerns about justice.

  • Ensure that no participant’s identity can be inferred from the data.
  • Avoid publishing granular data for small groups that might inadvertently identify individuals.
2. Beneficence (Promote Well-Being)
  • Focus analysis on actionable insights that genuinely improve course quality.
  • Ensure recommendations lead to better outcomes for learners, instructors, and the organization.
3. Autonomy
  • Respect participants’ autonomy by honoring their choice to provide anonymous feedback.
  • Avoid using data in ways that participants did not consent to.
4. Justice (Fairness)
  • Ensure that analysis does not privilege feedback from one demographic or subgroup over another.
  • Recognize and correct for potential survey response bias, ensuring fair evaluation across courses.
5. Explicability (Transparency and Accountability)
  • Document how data is processed, aggregated, and interpreted.
  • Communicate clearly with management about the limitations of the data and the ethical boundaries of its use.
Practical Guidelines for Ethical Analysis

Using these principles, here’s a roadmap for conducting the survey analysis responsibly:

1. Maintain Anonymity
  • Remove any identifiers (usernames, IP addresses) before analysis.
  • Aggregate responses at the course level, rather than attempting to analyze individual answers.
2. Protect Small-Group Privacy
  • If a course has very few participants, consider combining data with similar courses or withholding detailed breakdowns to avoid inadvertent identification.
3. Address Bias
  • Compare response rates across courses to identify skewed participation.
  • Weight responses or use statistical adjustments if certain groups are underrepresented.
4. Communicate Findings Transparently
  • Clearly explain to executive management that insights reflect aggregate trends and may not capture every nuance.
  • Highlight limitations, including potential response bias or missing data.
5. Focus on Ethical Decision-Making
  • Provide recommendations that are actionable but fair, avoiding any steps that could negatively impact specific individuals.
  • Ensure that the use of data aligns with the original purpose stated to participants: improving courses, not monitoring individual behavior.
Actionable Example

Suppose you find that one course consistently receives lower ratings for clarity and engagement. An ethical approach would be to:

  • Present findings at the course level without linking to individual responses.
  • Recommend instructional improvements such as updated materials, interactive exercises, or instructor training.
  • Monitor course changes over time to see if aggregate satisfaction improves, without ever revealing participant identities. This approach balances useful insights with respect for anonymity and fairness, demonstrating how ethics guide data analysis in practice.
Conclusion: Ethics as a Compass in Data Science

Analyzing course surveys may seem routine, but ethical principles are essential for guiding responsible action. By applying nonmaleficence, beneficence, autonomy, justice, and explicability, data scientists can:

  • Protect participant privacy and trust.
  • Deliver actionable insights that improve organizational outcomes.
  • Avoid unintended harm or discrimination in data interpretation.

Ethical vigilance ensures that data science not only generates value but also aligns with societal norms and organizational integrity. In an era where data is increasingly powerful, ethics acts as the compass guiding decisions from insight to impact. In short, ethical data science is not just about what we can do with data—it’s about what we should do.

Sources

Confronting the Challenges of Regulating Artificial Intelligence. Retrieved from https://ecollections.law.fiu.edu/lawreview/vol20/iss1/5/
title: Ethics and Confidentiality in Course Evaluations

content:
Mathjax

Las ecuaciones matemáticas se pueden renderizar usando la sintaxis de Mathjax con soporte de símbolos AMS.

Opcionalmente, habilita esto por página agregando mathjax: true a tu frontmatter.

Luego, usa $$ ... $$ en una línea por sí sola para renderizar una ecuación de bloque:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

La versión sin procesar es:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

Escribe ecuaciones en línea con \\( ... \\) , como \( x^n / y \) . ¡Es fácil!

Escribe ecuaciones en línea con `\\( ... \\)` , como \\( x^n / y \\) . ¡Es fácil!
title: Renderizar Matemáticas con Mathjax

content:
Mathjax

Les équations mathématiques peuvent être rendues en utilisant la syntaxe Mathjax avec le support des symboles AMS.

Activez ceci de manière optionnelle page par page en ajoutant mathjax: true à votre frontmatter.

Ensuite, utilisez $$ ... $$ sur une ligne par elle-même pour rendre une équation de bloc:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

La version brute est:

$$ | Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{1}} [A(x) = 1] - Pr_{x \leftarrow P_{2}} [A(x) = 1] | < \text{negligible} $$

Écrivez des équations en ligne avec \\( ... \\) , comme \( x^n / y \) . C’est facile!

Écrivez des équations en ligne avec `\\( ... \\)` , comme \\( x^n / y \\) . C'est facile!
title: Rendre les Mathématiques avec Mathjax

content:
Párrafo

Xerum, quo qui aut unt expliquam qui dolut labo. Aque venitatiusda cum, voluptionse latur sitiae dolessi aut parist aut dollo enim qui voluptate ma dolestendit peritin re plis aut quas inctum laceat est volestemque commosa as cus endigna tectur, offic to cor sequas etum rerum idem sintibus eiur? Quianimin porecus evelectur, cum que nis nust voloribus ratem aut omnimi, sitatur? Quiatem. Nam, omnis sum am facea corem alique molestrunt et eos evelece arcillit ut aut eos eos nus, sin conecerem erum fuga. Ri oditatquam, ad quibus unda veliamenimin cusam et facea ipsamus es exerum sitate dolores editium rerore eost, temped molorro ratiae volorro te reribus dolorer sperchicium faceata tiustia prat.

Itatur? Quiatae cullecum rem ent aut odis in re eossequodi nonsequ idebis ne sapicia is sinveli squiatum, core et que aut hariosam ex eat.

Citas en bloque

El elemento blockquote representa contenido que se cita de otra fuente, opcionalmente con una cita que debe estar dentro de un elemento footer o cite, y opcionalmente con cambios en línea como anotaciones y abreviaturas.

Cita en bloque sin atribución

Tiam, ad mint andaepu dandae nostion secatur sequo quae. Nota que puedes usar sintaxis de Markdown dentro de una cita en bloque.

Cita en bloque con atribución

No comuniques compartiendo memoria, comparte memoria comunicando. — Rob Pike1

Tablas

Las tablas no forman parte de la especificación principal de Markdown, pero Hugo las admite de forma predeterminada.

Nombre Edad
Bob 27
Alice 23

Markdown en línea dentro de tablas

Cursiva Negrita Código
cursiva negrita código

Bloques de código

Bloque de código con acentos graves

<!doctype html>
<html lang="es">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8" />
    <title>Ejemplo de documento HTML5</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>Prueba</p>
  </body>
</html>

Bloque de código con cuatro espacios

<!doctype html>
<html lang="es">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <title>Ejemplo de documento HTML5</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>Prueba</p>
  </body>
</html>

Bloque de código con el shortcode de resaltado interno de Hugo

<!doctype html>
<html lang="es">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <title>Ejemplo de documento HTML5</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>Prueba</p>
  </body>
</html>

Tipos de lista

Lista ordenada

  1. Primer elemento
  2. Segundo elemento
  3. Tercer elemento

Lista desordenada

  • Elemento de lista
  • Otro elemento
  • Y otro elemento

Lista anidada

  • Fruta
    • Manzana
    • Naranja
    • Plátano
  • Lácteos
    • Leche
    • Queso

Otros elementos — abbr, sub, sup, kbd, mark

GIF es un formato de imagen de mapa de bits.

H2O

Xn + Yn = Zn

Presiona CTRL+ALT+Supr para finalizar la sesión.

La mayoría de las salamandras son nocturnas y cazan insectos, gusanos y otras criaturas pequeñas.

  1. La cita anterior se extrae de la charla de Rob Pike durante Gopherfest, 18 de noviembre de 2015. ↩︎
title: Sintaxis de Markdown

content:
Paragraphe

Xerum, quo qui aut unt expliquam qui dolut labo. Aque venitatiusda cum, voluptionse latur sitiae dolessi aut parist aut dollo enim qui voluptate ma dolestendit peritin re plis aut quas inctum laceat est volestemque commosa as cus endigna tectur, offic to cor sequas etum rerum idem sintibus eiur? Quianimin porecus evelectur, cum que nis nust voloribus ratem aut omnimi, sitatur? Quiatem. Nam, omnis sum am facea corem alique molestrunt et eos evelece arcillit ut aut eos eos nus, sin conecerem erum fuga. Ri oditatquam, ad quibus unda veliamenimin cusam et facea ipsamus es exerum sitate dolores editium rerore eost, temped molorro ratiae volorro te reribus dolorer sperchicium faceata tiustia prat.

Itatur? Quiatae cullecum rem ent aut odis in re eossequodi nonsequ idebis ne sapicia is sinveli squiatum, core et que aut hariosam ex eat.

Citations

L’élément blockquote représente le contenu cité d’une autre source, éventuellement avec une citation qui doit se trouver dans un élément footer ou cite, et éventuellement avec des modifications en ligne telles que des annotations et des abréviations.

Citation sans attribution

Tiam, ad mint andaepu dandae nostion secatur sequo quae. Notez que vous pouvez utiliser la syntaxe Markdown dans une citation.

Citation avec attribution

Ne communiquez pas en partageant la mémoire, partagez la mémoire en communiquant. — Rob Pike1

Tableaux

Les tableaux ne font pas partie de la spécification Markdown de base, mais Hugo les prend en charge par défaut.

Nom Âge
Bob 27
Alice 23

Markdown en ligne dans les tableaux

Italique Gras Code
italique gras code

Blocs de code

Bloc de code avec des backticks

<!doctype html>
<html lang="fr">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8" />
    <title>Exemple de document HTML5</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>Test</p>
  </body>
</html>

Bloc de code indenté de quatre espaces

<!doctype html>
<html lang="fr">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <title>Exemple de document HTML5</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>Test</p>
  </body>
</html>

Bloc de code avec le shortcode de surbrillance intégré de Hugo

<!doctype html>
<html lang="fr">
  <head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <title>Exemple de document HTML5</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <p>Test</p>
  </body>
</html>

Types de listes

Liste ordonnée

  1. Premier élément
  2. Deuxième élément
  3. Troisième élément

Liste non ordonnée

  • Élément de liste
  • Un autre élément
  • Et un autre élément

Liste imbriquée

  • Fruits
    • Pomme
    • Orange
    • Banane
  • Produits laitiers
    • Lait
    • Fromage

Autres éléments — abbr, sub, sup, kbd, mark

GIF est un format d’image bitmap.

H2O

Xn + Yn = Zn

Appuyez sur CTRL+ALT+Suppr pour terminer la session.

La plupart des salamandres sont nocturnes et chassent les insectes, les vers et autres petites créatures.

  1. La citation ci-dessus est extraite de la conférence de Rob Pike lors de Gopherfest, le 18 novembre 2015. ↩︎
title: Syntaxe Markdown

content:
Los emoji se pueden habilitar en un proyecto Hugo de varias maneras. :zap:

Soporte de Emoji

La función emojify se puede llamar directamente en las plantillas o Shortcodes en línea.

Para habilitar emoji globalmente, establece enableEmoji en true en la configuración de tu sitio y luego puedes escribir códigos abreviados de emoji directamente en los archivos de contenido; por ejemplo:

La Hoja de trucos de Emoji es una referencia útil para los códigos abreviados de emoji.

N.B. Los pasos anteriores habilitan los caracteres y secuencias de emoji del Estándar Unicode en Hugo, sin embargo, la representación de estos glifos depende del navegador y la plataforma. Para estilizar los emoji, puedes usar una fuente de emoji de terceros o una pila de fuentes; por ejemplo:

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}
title: Soporte de Emoji

content:
Les emoji peuvent être activés dans un projet Hugo de plusieurs façons. :zap:

Support des Emoji

La fonction emojify peut être appelée directement dans les modèles ou les Shortcodes en ligne.

Pour activer les emoji globalement, définissez enableEmoji sur true dans la configuration de votre site, puis vous pouvez taper les codes abrégés d’emoji directement dans les fichiers de contenu; par exemple :

La Feuille de triche Emoji est une référence utile pour les codes abrégés d’emoji.

N.B. Les étapes ci-dessus activent les caractères et séquences emoji du standard Unicode dans Hugo, cependant le rendu de ces glyphes dépend du navigateur et de la plateforme. Pour styliser les emoji, vous pouvez utiliser une police emoji tierce ou une pile de polices; par exemple :

.emoji {
  font-family: Apple Color Emoji, Segoe UI Emoji, NotoColorEmoji, Segoe UI Symbol, Android Emoji, EmojiSymbols;
}
title: Support des Emoji

Victa caducifer, malo vulnere contra dicere aurato, ludit regale, voca! Retorsit colit est profanae esse virescere furit nec; iaculi matertera et visa est, viribus. Divesque creatis, tecta novat collumque vulnus est, parvas. Faces illo pepulere tempus adest. Tendit flamma, ab opes virum sustinet, sidus sequendo urbis.

Iubar proles corpore raptos vero auctor imperium; sed et huic: manus caeli Lelegas tu lux. Verbis obstitit intus oblectamina fixis linguisque ausus sperare Echionides cornuaque tenent clausit possit. Omnia putatur. Praeteritae refert ausus; ferebant e primus lora nutat, vici quae mea ipse. Et iter nil spectatae vulnus haerentia iuste et exercebat, sui et.

Eurytus Hector, materna ipsumque ut Politen, nec, nate, ignari, vernum cohaesit sequitur. Vel malis latens indetectus caelum supremos missus, nec nil videt exiguo! Manifestum, exasperat nova tela, ora, decipit ipse; fuerat gelidis sua.

Mentefque cum suae pelle

Viscera tot vagina, mihi est Theseus! Obstruat pede! Concita tamen, cum et vulnera croceo aegide tetigere deducit. Est quis vultus fata, scelus paratae inter agmen spectat fuit, Thisbe vulnera vellet laniarat, arva paludibus!

Dixit exarsit nocens, dextra secum ira columbae: Proponas, in pinus et alis dea atque. Aeneas, certum possent, temeraria, umeris colla in quoque. Interea fugit inpediunt nocet.

Praecipites nescio Titania tetigit, nunc volentem sua pectora nec, exstinctum Pylius undas. Agro. Dumque in est moenia vidi, vinci dat acervos precatur bracchia et modo et quos cum illas solvet, dum fecit.
title: Texto de Relleno
